problem_spec.description:
Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

variables.name: base_SMILES
variables.description: Base encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: ligand_SMILES
variables.description: Ligand encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: solvent_SMILES
variables.description: Solvent encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: concentration
variables.description: Discrete concentration levels observed in the dataset

variables.name: temperature
variables.description: Discrete temperature levels observed in the dataset

constraints: Only propose conditions that correspond to rows present in the DAR dataset.
problem_spec.raw_description:
Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

kernel_config.rationale: The campaign has 10 consecutive ModelFittingErrors with standard Matern-5/2 kernel on one-hot encoding. The mixed_sum_product kernel is specifically designed for encoded mixed spaces with uncertain interaction structure [KB:kernel_catalog]. It provides a weighted blend of additive (main effects) and multiplicative (interaction) behavior, which is critical since the data shows strong cation-ligand interaction effects (Cs+/XPhos=89%, Cs+/BrettPhos=42%, K+/BrettPhos=79%) that violate simple additive models. This kernel was explicitly recommended in the last reconfiguration attempt but may not have been properly applied. The one-hot encoding (26D) with 5 categorical variables creates a space where main effects and interactions are both present; mixed_sum_product is the only kernel option that explicitly balances these. If this fails, we will need to abandon one-hot encoding entirely for SMKBO with molecular fingerprints.

bo_config.rationale: Log Expected Improvement is the default stable choice for single-objective BO [KB:af_catalog]. With 15 iterations remaining and a known optimum at 89.17%, we need reliable improvement-based selection. UCB was tried in version 2 without resolving the model fitting issues. LogEI provides better numerical stability than classic EI and does not require tuning a beta parameter like UCB. Given the surrogate has been failing, we need an acquisition function that is robust to model uncertainty - LogEI's log-space transformation provides this. We are in exploitation phase (best=89.17% found), but given recent catastrophic failures (4.74%, 0%, 0.07%), we need the model to guide us back to high-yielding regions, which LogEI will do by exploiting regions near the current best while still exploring uncertain high-potential areas.

kernel_config.rationale: The campaign has 10 consecutive ModelFittingErrors with standard Matern-5/2 kernel on one-hot encoding. The mixed_sum_product kernel is specifically designed for encoded mixed spaces with uncertain interaction structure [KB:kernel_catalog]. It provides a weighted blend of additive (main effects) and multiplicative (interaction) behavior, which is critical since the data shows strong cation-ligand interaction effects (Cs+/XPhos=89%, Cs+/BrettPhos=42%, K+/BrettPhos=79%) that violate simple additive models. This kernel was explicitly recommended in the last reconfiguration attempt but may not have been properly applied. The one-hot encoding (26D) with 5 categorical variables creates a space where main effects and interactions are both present; mixed_sum_product is the only kernel option that explicitly balances these. If this fails, we will need to abandon one-hot encoding entirely for SMKBO with molecular fingerprints.

fallbacks: ModelFittingError: All attempts to fit the model have failed.

hypotheses.text: Electron-rich dienes paired with electron-poor dienophiles will yield higher DAR product yields due to enhanced frontier molecular orbital (FMO) interactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: The Diels-Alder reaction is a [4+2] cycloaddition where the HOMO of the diene interacts with the LUMO of the dienophile. Electron-donating groups (EDGs) on the diene raise its HOMO energy, while electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) on the dienophile lower its LUMO energy, reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap and accelerating the reaction.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Combinations with strong EDG-substituted dienes (e.g., alkoxy, amino) and EWG-substituted dienophiles (e.g., carbonyl, nitro) will show systematically higher yields than mismatched or neutral combinations.

hypotheses.text: Steric bulk on the dienophile will negatively impact yield due to approach trajectory constraints in the transition state.
hypotheses.mechanism: The DAR requires a suprafacial-suprafacial approach with specific geometric constraints. Bulky substituents on the dienophile create steric hindrance in the endo/exo transition states, increasing activation energy and potentially favoring side reactions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Dienophiles with bulky substituents (e.g., t-butyl, ortho-substituted aromatics) will show lower yields compared to less hindered alternatives (e.g., methyl, hydrogen).

hypotheses.text: Cyclic dienes will exhibit higher reactivity and yields than acyclic dienes due to locked s-cis conformation.
hypotheses.mechanism: The DAR requires the diene to adopt an s-cis conformation. Cyclic dienes (e.g., cyclopentadiene, cyclohexadiene) are locked in this geometry, eliminating the entropic penalty of conformational pre-organization and accelerating the reaction rate.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cyclic diene variants will consistently outperform acyclic dienes, with 5-membered rings showing optimal reactivity.

hypotheses.text: Ortho/para directing substituent patterns on aromatic dienes will create regioselective preferences that correlate with yield optimization.
hypotheses.mechanism: Substituent positioning affects both electronic distribution and steric accessibility. Ortho and para positions on aromatic dienes can stabilize the developing positive charge in the transition state through resonance, while meta positions offer less stabilization.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Diene substitutions at ortho/para positions relative to the reacting diene system will yield higher products than meta-substituted variants.

hypotheses.text: Heteroatom-containing dienophiles (azadienophiles) may show unique reactivity profiles compared to all-carbon dienophiles.
hypotheses.mechanism: Nitrogen or oxygen substitution in the dienophile alters LUMO energy and polarity. Azadienophiles can participate in hetero-Diels-Alder reactions with different transition state stabilization and potential for Lewis acid catalysis enhancement.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Azadienophiles will form a distinct cluster in yield space, potentially showing higher yields with specific diene partners or requiring different optimization strategies.

hypotheses.text: BrettPhos ligand with Cs+ cation will achieve >90% yield by combining electron-rich biaryl architecture with superior cation coordination
hypotheses.mechanism: BrettPhos's 2,6-dimethoxy substitution increases electron density at Pd center, accelerating oxidative addition; Cs+ provides optimal cation-π and coordination interactions with the biaryl backbone that K+ cannot match. The iteration 9 failure (16.39%) was K+-driven, not ligand-driven.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/acetate/BrettPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will yield 90-95%, exceeding current XPhos optimum by 1-6%

hypotheses.text: RuPhos ligand (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) will match or exceed XPhos performance due to optimized steric-electronic balance
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos's isopropoxy substituents provide stronger electron donation than XPhos's isopropyl groups while maintaining similar steric bulk (Cy2P). The alkoxy groups may also improve solubility in polar DMF solvent.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/acetate/RuPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will yield 88-92%, statistically equivalent or superior to XPhos (89.17%)

hypotheses.text: SPhos ligand will underperform XPhos due to insufficient steric bulk for this substrate combination
hypotheses.mechanism: SPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-dimethoxybiphenyl) has smaller ortho substituents (OMe vs iPr) than BrettPhos, potentially leading to catalyst deactivation via aggregation or insufficient stabilization of the Pd(II) oxidative addition intermediate.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/acetate/SPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will yield 75-85%, below XPhos benchmark

hypotheses.text: The 0.057M/105°C conditions are ligand-transferable—optimal for XPhos and will remain optimal for alternative ligands
hypotheses.mechanism: Concentration and temperature optima are determined by mass transport, solubility, and thermal stability constraints that are largely ligand-independent. The systematic mapping with XPhos revealed 105°C as the kinetic optimum and 0.057M as a robust concentration; these should transfer to other biaryl phosphine ligands.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: For any biaryl phosphine ligand (BrettPhos, RuPhos, SPhos), the yield ranking across concentration-temperature combinations will mirror XPhos: 0.057M/105°C ≥ 0.1M/105°C > 0.057M/120°C > other combinations

hypotheses.text: K+/acetate/XPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve 70-80% yield, confirming K+ is viable but inferior to Cs+
hypotheses.mechanism: Iteration 10 showed K+/pivalate/XPhos/DMF = 61%; switching to acetate (superior base) should recover 10-20% yield. This tests the isolated cation effect without pivalate penalty, establishing the Cs+ premium at ~10-20% absolute yield.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: K+/acetate/XPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will yield 70-80%, vs 89% for Cs+ equivalent

hypotheses.text: Ligand-cation compatibility is a critical, non-additive interaction: XPhos requires Cs+ for optimal performance while BrettPhos requires K+ for viability.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 2,6-dimethoxy substitution pattern on BrettPhos creates a coordination environment that is destabilized by Cs+ due to ionic radius mismatch or competing coordination with the methoxy oxygens. Cs+ (167 pm) may bridge between the phosphine and methoxy oxygens, disrupting Pd binding. K+ (138 pm) provides optimal size match for BrettPhos's pocket. Conversely, XPhos's isopropyl groups create a more open pocket favoring Cs+ coordination. This is a specific ligand-cation pairing effect not captured by one-hot encoding.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: K+/BrettPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve 75-85% yield, recovering from the Cs+/BrettPhos failure (42%) and approaching the K+/BrettPhos/0.1M result (79%).

hypotheses.text: RuPhos ligand (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) will match or exceed XPhos performance due to optimized steric-electronic balance.
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos's isopropoxy substituents provide stronger electron donation than XPhos's isopropyl groups while maintaining similar steric bulk (Cy2P). The alkoxy groups may improve solubility in polar DMF solvent. Unlike BrettPhos's dimethoxy which interacts problematically with Cs+, RuPhos's diisopropoxy may provide better Cs+ compatibility.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/acetate/RuPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will yield 88-92%, statistically equivalent or superior to XPhos (89.17%).

hypotheses.text: SPhos ligand will underperform XPhos due to insufficient steric bulk for this substrate combination.
hypotheses.mechanism: SPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-dimethoxybiphenyl) has smaller ortho substituents (OMe vs iPr) than BrettPhos, potentially leading to catalyst deactivation via aggregation or insufficient stabilization of the Pd(II) oxidative addition intermediate. Given BrettPhos's catastrophic failure with Cs+, SPhos may show similar cation sensitivity.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/acetate/SPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will yield 60-75%, below XPhos benchmark. K+/SPhos may be preferred.

hypotheses.text: The Cs+/XPhos/DMF/0.057M/105°C system (89.17%) represents a local optimum; exceeding 90% requires exploring alternative bases or solvent modifications.
hypotheses.mechanism: The campaign has thoroughly mapped the Cs+/XPhos parameter space (concentration 0.057-0.153M, temperature 105-120°C, solvents DMF/butyl acetate) without exceeding 89.17%. The BrettPhos failure suggests ligand variation within biaryl phosphines may not provide breakthrough yields. Alternative bases (carbonate, phosphate) or solvent additives may be required.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Systematic testing of alternative bases (K2CO3, NaOtBu) with Cs+/XPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will identify if base variation can exceed 90%.

hypotheses.text: The GP model's failure to predict the Cs+/BrettPhos incompatibility indicates the kernel cannot capture ligand-cation interaction effects.
hypotheses.mechanism: One-hot encoding treats all categorical levels as orthogonal, preventing the model from learning that specific ligand-cation pairs (BrettPhos-Cs+, XPhos-K+) have non-additive, synergistic or antagonistic effects. A structured kernel (SMKBO) separating ligand, cation, and solvent subspaces with explicit interaction terms is needed.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguring to SMKBO with separate kernels for cation, ligand, solvent vs concentration/temperature will improve predictive accuracy for unexplored ligand-cation combinations from ~45% (current) to >70%.

hypotheses.text: Ligand-cation compatibility is a critical, non-additive interaction: XPhos requires Cs+ for optimal performance while BrettPhos requires K+ for viability.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 2,6-dimethoxy substitution pattern on BrettPhos creates a coordination environment that is destabilized by Cs+ due to ionic radius mismatch or competing coordination with the methoxy oxygens. Cs+ (167 pm) may bridge between the phosphine and methoxy oxygens, disrupting Pd binding. K+ (138 pm) provides optimal size match for BrettPhos's pocket. Conversely, XPhos's isopropyl groups create a more open pocket favoring Cs+ coordination. This is a specific ligand-cation pairing effect not captured by one-hot encoding.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: K+/BrettPhos/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve 75-85% yield, recovering from the Cs+/BrettPhos failure (42%) and approaching the K+/BrettPhos/0.1M result (79%).

hypotheses.text: RuPhos ligand (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) will achieve >90% yield with Cs+ cation due to optimal steric/electronic balance.
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines the electron-rich alkoxy backbone of BrettPhos with the steric bulk of dicyclohexylphosphino groups. The isopropoxy groups (vs methoxy in BrettPhos) provide better Cs+ tolerance due to increased steric protection of the coordination pocket. The dicyclohexylphosphino groups provide stronger electron donation than XPhos's biaryl phosphine, accelerating oxidative addition without the BrettPhos-Cs+ incompatibility.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/RuPhos/DMF/0.057M/105°C will achieve 90-95% yield, breaking the 89.17% barrier that has persisted for 8 iterations with XPhos.

hypotheses.text: The Cs+/BrettPhos incompatibility is solvent-mediated: DMF's strong coordination to Cs+ exacerbates the ligand destabilization.
hypotheses.mechanism: DMF is a strongly coordinating polar aprotic solvent that forms stable complexes with Cs+. When combined with BrettPhos's methoxy oxygens, this creates a competitive coordination environment where Cs+ is pulled between DMF and the ligand, disrupting the Pd-ligand complex. In a less coordinating solvent (e.g., butyl acetate), Cs+ may bind more selectively to BrettPhos, potentially recovering performance.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/BrettPhos/butyl acetate/0.057M/105°C will achieve >70% yield, significantly better than the DMF result (42%), testing whether solvent choice can rescue the ligand-cation pair.

hypotheses.text: Concentration-temperature coupling creates a broader optimum plateau than previously recognized: 0.1M/120°C may match 0.057M/105°C performance.
hypotheses.mechanism: The reaction rate is limited by both mass transport (concentration-dependent) and activation energy (temperature-dependent). At higher concentration, increased collision frequency compensates for slightly lower temperature; at higher temperature, faster kinetics compensate for lower concentration. The Cs+/acetate/XPhos/DMF system operates in a flat region of the energy landscape where multiple (concentration, temperature) combinations achieve equivalent yields.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/acetate/XPhos/DMF at 0.1M/120°C will achieve 87-89% yield, statistically equivalent to the 0.057M/105°C optimum (89.17%), confirming process flexibility.

hypotheses.text: The GP model with Matern-5/2 kernel on one-hot encoding is systematically failing to capture interaction effects, requiring kernel reconfiguration to SMKBO.
hypotheses.mechanism: One-hot encoding treats all categorical variables as orthogonal dimensions, destroying the structural similarity information in SMILES strings. The Matern-5/2 kernel assumes smoothness in this 26-dimensional space that does not exist—Cs+/XPhos and Cs+/BrettPhos are 'distant' in one-hot space but chemically related. The model cannot learn that K+/BrettPhos success predicts nothing about Cs+/BrettPhos performance. SMKBO with separate kernels for SMILES (Tanimoto) vs continuous variables would capture ligand similarity and cation-ligand interactions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguring to SMKBO with Tanimoto kernel for ligand SMILES and separate handling of cation-ligand interactions will reduce prediction error from >30% (iteration 16: predicted 45%, observed 42%) to <15% for the next 5 iterations.

hypotheses.text: SMKBO with separate Tanimoto kernels for ligand/cation/solvent SMILES will reduce prediction error from >30% to <15% by capturing structural similarity and explicit interaction effects
hypotheses.mechanism: The current one-hot encoding destroys chemical structure information, treating XPhos and BrettPhos as orthogonal despite shared biaryl phosphine architecture. SMKBO uses Tanimoto similarity on molecular fingerprints, enabling the model to learn that ligands with similar scaffolds have correlated performance. Separate kernels for cation, ligand, and solvent allow explicit modeling of their interactions—critical since Cs+/BrettPhos (42%) and K+/BrettPhos (79%) show opposite trends to the general Cs+>K+ rule. The kernel structure will encode domain knowledge: cation-ligand compatibility is a learned interaction term, not assumed additive.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After SMKBO reconfiguration, predictions for the next 5 unexplored ligand-cation combinations will have mean absolute error <15% vs >30% with current Matern-5/2. Specifically, RuPhos/Cs+ prediction will be 85-95% (vs current unreliable extrapolation).

hypotheses.text: RuPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) with Cs+/DMF/0.057M/105°C will achieve 90-95% yield, breaking the 89.17% XPhos barrier
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines BrettPhos's electron-rich alkoxy backbone with XPhos-compatible steric bulk. The isopropoxy groups (vs BrettPhos's methoxy) provide steric protection that prevents the Cs+-mediated destabilization observed in iteration 16. The dicyclohexylphosphino groups are less electron-rich than BrettPhos's di-tert-butylphosphino, avoiding excessive electron density that may disrupt Pd(0) stability with Cs+. This represents the optimal balance: sufficient electron donation for oxidative addition, moderated to prevent cation-specific decomposition.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/RuPhos/DMF/0.057M/105°C will yield 90-95%, becoming the new campaign best. The SMKBO model will predict this accurately (within 5%) due to structural similarity to XPhos (biaryl scaffold) and BrettPhos (alkoxy substitution).

hypotheses.text: SPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-dimethoxybiphenyl) will achieve 85-90% with Cs+, serving as a BrettPhos-RuPhos intermediate validation
hypotheses.mechanism: SPhos has dimethoxy substitution like BrettPhos but dicyclohexylphosphino like RuPhos. Testing SPhos isolates the alkoxy vs phosphino electronic effects: if SPhos/Cs+ fails like BrettPhos/Cs+ (42%), the methoxy groups are the Cs+ incompatibility source; if SPhos/Cs+ succeeds (85%+), the di-tert-butylphosphino in BrettPhos is the problem. This disentangles H2's mechanistic claim about isopropoxy protection.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/SPhos/DMF/0.057M/105°C will yield 85-90% if phosphino group identity dominates, or <60% if methoxy groups cause Cs+ incompatibility. Result will validate or refute the isopropoxy-protection mechanism in H2.

hypotheses.text: The 0.057M optimum is ligand-specific: RuPhos may tolerate 0.1M without yield loss due to superior catalyst stability
hypotheses.mechanism: XPhos's 89.17% optimum at 0.057M may reflect catalyst decomposition at higher concentration. RuPhos's enhanced steric protection (isopropoxy + dicyclohexyl) could stabilize the Pd complex against concentration-dependent deactivation pathways (clustering, precipitation). If true, RuPhos enables process flexibility: 0.1M for throughput or 0.057M for maximum yield.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/RuPhos/DMF at 0.1M/105°C will achieve 88-92% vs 90-95% at 0.057M, showing <5% yield penalty for 75% concentration increase. XPhos at 0.1M shows 87.83% (iteration 11), a 1.3% penalty.

hypotheses.text: BrettPhos/Cs+ incompatibility is temperature-dependent: lower temperature (90°C) will recover >70% yield by reducing Cs+-mediated decomposition kinetics
hypotheses.mechanism: Iteration 16's 42% yield at 105°C suggests thermal activation of a decomposition pathway unique to BrettPhos/Cs+. The methoxy-Cs+ coordination may form an unstable complex that decomposes above 100°C. Lowering temperature to 90°C (validated robust for XPhos, 79.33%) could suppress this pathway while maintaining sufficient activity. This tests whether BrettPhos is permanently incompatible or conditionally incompatible.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/BrettPhos/DMF/0.057M/90°C will achieve 70-80% yield, vs 42% at 105°C. If successful, BrettPhos remains viable for low-temperature applications; if <50%, BrettPhos is permanently excluded from Cs+ systems.

hypotheses.text: SMKBO with separate Tanimoto kernels for ligand/cation/solvent SMILES will reduce prediction error from >30% to <15% by capturing structural similarity and explicit interaction effects
hypotheses.mechanism: The current one-hot encoding destroys chemical structure information, treating XPhos and BrettPhos as orthogonal despite shared biaryl phosphine architecture. SMKBO uses Tanimoto similarity on molecular fingerprints, enabling the model to learn that ligands with similar scaffolds have correlated performance. Separate kernels for cation, ligand, and solvent allow explicit modeling of their interactions—critical since Cs+/BrettPhos (42%) and K+/BrettPhos (79%) show opposite trends to the general Cs+>K+ rule. The kernel structure will encode domain knowledge: cation-ligand compatibility is a learned interaction term, not assumed additive.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After 5 iterations with SMKBO, the model will achieve <15% MAE on held-out ligand-cation combinations, compared to >30% MAE with one-hot/Matern-5/2. Specifically, SMKBO will correctly predict that BrettPhos performs better with K+ than Cs+, while XPhos shows the opposite pattern.

hypotheses.text: RuPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) with Cs+ cation will achieve 88-92% yield in DMF at 0.057M/105°C, matching or exceeding XPhos performance
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines the proven biaryl phosphine scaffold (successful with XPhos, BrettPhos) with 2,6-diisopropoxy substitution that provides both steric bulk and electron donation. Unlike BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups which may coordinate Cs+ too strongly (causing the 42% failure), RuPhos's alkoxy groups are less basic and less likely to cause cation-specific deactivation. The isopropoxy groups are also more electron-withdrawing than BrettPhos's dimethylamino, potentially providing better catalyst stability. Cs+ compatibility is predicted based on XPhos/Cs+ success (89.17%) and the hypothesis that alkoxy-substituted biaryl phosphines avoid the BrettPhos-Cs+ incompatibility.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos/Cs+/acetate/DMF/0.057M/105°C will yield 88-92%, compared to 89.17% for XPhos/Cs+ and 42.43% for BrettPhos/Cs+. If successful, RuPhos becomes the new lead ligand; if 75-85%, it matches XPhos; if <60%, the biaryl phosphine scaffold has intrinsic Cs+ limitations beyond BrettPhos.

hypotheses.text: Ligand-cation compatibility follows a discrete matching rule: biaryl phosphines with alkyl/alkoxy substitution (XPhos, RuPhos) require Cs+; biaryl phosphines with amino substitution (BrettPhos) require K+; monodentate phosphines (tBu3P) are incompatible with both cations for high yields
hypotheses.mechanism: The iteration 16-17 disasters reveal that cation-ligand pairing is not simply 'Cs+ is best' but depends on ligand electronics and coordination geometry. BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups are strong sigma donors that may over-stabilize Pd(0) with Cs+, preventing oxidative addition; K+'s weaker coordination allows proper catalytic turnover. XPhos's isopropyl groups provide moderate electron donation compatible with Cs+. tBu3P's extreme steric bulk (cone angle ~182°) and single coordination site cannot stabilize the catalytic cycle regardless of cation. This discrete matching explains the counterintuitive results: BrettPhos/K+ (79%) > BrettPhos/Cs+ (42%), XPhos/Cs+ (89%) > XPhos/K+ (61%).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing RuPhos/Cs+ (predicted 88-92%), SPhos/Cs+ (predicted 85-90%), and BrettPhos/K+ reconfirmation (predicted 75-80%) will validate the matching rule. If SPhos/Cs+ fails (<60%), the rule is specific to XPhos/RuPhos architecture; if all alkyl/alkoxy biaryls succeed with Cs+, the rule generalizes.

hypotheses.text: BrettPhos/Cs+ incompatibility in DMF can be rescued by switching to a less coordinating solvent (butyl acetate or valeronitrile), achieving >70% yield by reducing cation-ligand-solvent three-body interactions
hypotheses.mechanism: The 42% BrettPhos/Cs+/DMF failure may result from a three-body interaction: Cs+ coordinates to both BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups AND DMF's carbonyl oxygen, creating an over-stabilized, catalytically inactive complex. DMF is a strong Lewis base (donor number 26.6) and may compete with substrate for Pd coordination when Cs+ is present. Switching to a weaker donor solvent (butyl acetate, donor number ~17; valeronitrile, donor number ~15) reduces this competition, allowing the BrettPhos/Cs+ pair to function. This hypothesis explains why BrettPhos/K+/DMF succeeded (79%): K+ is a weaker Lewis acid and does not over-coordinate with DMF.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BrettPhos/Cs+/acetate/butyl acetate/0.057M/105°C will yield 70-80%, compared to 42% in DMF. If successful, this opens a new optimization dimension: solvent selection depends on cation-ligand pair. If <50%, the BrettPhos-Cs+ incompatibility is intrinsic, not solvent-mediated.

hypotheses.text: The 0.057M/105°C optimum for XPhos/Cs+/DMF is ligand-specific: BrettPhos and RuPhos will show different optimal concentration-temperature combinations due to distinct rate-limiting steps
hypotheses.mechanism: XPhos's optimum (0.057M/105°C) reflects a balance between oxidative addition (favored by lower temperature, higher concentration) and reductive elimination (favored by higher temperature, lower concentration). BrettPhos's electron-rich dimethylamino groups accelerate oxidative addition, potentially shifting the optimum to lower temperature (90°C) and higher concentration (0.1M) to favor reductive elimination. RuPhos's alkoxy groups provide intermediate electronics, potentially showing broad optimality across 0.05-0.1M and 100-110°C. The current fixed 0.057M/105°C testing may suboptimize non-XPhos ligands.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos will achieve maximum yield at 0.1M/110°C (88-92%) compared to 0.057M/105°C (82-86%), while BrettPhos/K+ will optimize at 0.1M/90°C (82-86%) compared to 0.057M/105°C (75-79%). Testing these alternative conditions will reveal whether the XPhos optimum is globally optimal or ligand-specific.

hypotheses.text: SMKBO with Tanimoto kernels for ligand/base/solvent SMILES and separate handling of continuous variables will resolve the 7-iteration GP failure and enable reliable prediction of cation-ligand interaction effects
hypotheses.mechanism: The current one-hot encoding (26D) with Matern-5/2 kernel assumes smoothness in a space where chemical similarity is destroyed. XPhos and BrettPhos are 'distant' in one-hot space despite sharing biaryl phosphine architecture. The kernel matrix becomes ill-conditioned with limited data (n=20), causing ModelFittingError. SMKBO uses Tanimoto similarity on molecular fingerprints, capturing that ligands with similar scaffolds have correlated performance. Separate kernels for cation, ligand, solvent, and continuous variables allow explicit modeling of interactions—critical since Cs+/BrettPhos (42%) and K+/BrettPhos (79%) show opposite trends to the general Cs+>K+ rule. The kernel structure encodes domain knowledge: cation-ligand compatibility is a learned interaction term, not assumed additive.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After SMKBO reconfiguration, the model will successfully fit without ModelFittingError for 5 consecutive iterations. Prediction MAE on held-out ligand-cation combinations will improve from >30% (current) to <15%. Specifically, SMKBO will correctly predict that: (a) any K+ combination yields <80%, (b) BrettPhos/Cs+ underperforms BrettPhos/K+, (c) RuPhos/Cs+ performs similarly to XPhos/Cs+.

hypotheses.text: RuPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) with Cs+/acetate/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve 88-92% yield, providing the final opportunity to exceed the 89.17% XPhos benchmark
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines the proven biaryl phosphine scaffold with 2,6-diisopropoxy substitution—electron-rich but less basic than BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups. The iteration 16 disaster (BrettPhos/Cs+ = 42%) suggests BrettPhos's amino groups cause cation-specific deactivation, possibly by over-coordinating Cs+ and stabilizing Pd(0) too strongly. RuPhos's alkoxy groups avoid this while maintaining electron donation for catalyst stability. Cs+ compatibility is predicted based on XPhos/Cs+ success (89.17%) and the hypothesis that alkoxy-substituted biaryl phosphines avoid the BrettPhos-Cs+ incompatibility. This is the only remaining untested biaryl phosphine with distinct electronics in the dataset.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos/Cs+/acetate/DMF/0.057M/105°C will yield 88-92%. If successful, RuPhos becomes the new lead ligand; if 80-87%, it matches but does not exceed XPhos; if <75%, the biaryl phosphine scaffold has intrinsic Cs+ limitations beyond BrettPhos, and 89.17% is the practical maximum achievable with ligand optimization alone.

hypotheses.text: Cation-ligand compatibility follows a discrete matching rule: alkyl/alkoxy-substituted biaryl phosphines (XPhos, RuPhos) require Cs+ for >85% yields; amino-substituted biaryl phosphines (BrettPhos) show inverted preference (K+ > Cs+); monodentate phosphines are incompatible with both cations for high yields
hypotheses.mechanism: The iteration 16-20 results reveal that cation-ligand pairing is not simply 'Cs+ is best' but depends on ligand electronics. BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups are strong sigma donors that may over-stabilize Pd(0) with Cs+, preventing oxidative addition; K+'s weaker coordination allows proper catalytic turnover. XPhos's isopropyl groups provide moderate electron donation compatible with Cs+. Monodentate phosphines (tBu3P, PCy3, PPh3) fail regardless of cation due to insufficient catalyst stability. This discrete matching explains: BrettPhos/K+ (79%) > BrettPhos/Cs+ (42%), XPhos/Cs+ (89%) > XPhos/K+ (61%), and monodentate/K+ (<30%).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing RuPhos/Cs+ (predicted 88-92%) will validate the alkyl/alkoxy biaryl-Cs+ matching rule. If successful, the rule generalizes to all alkyl/alkoxy biaryls; if RuPhos/Cs+ fails (<75%), the rule is specific to XPhos architecture only. No further K+ testing is warranted given 5 consecutive failures across all ligand classes.

hypotheses.text: DMF is uniquely optimal among available solvents; nitrile solvents (valeronitrile) and ester solvents (butyl acetate) cannot match DMF performance regardless of cation-ligand combination
hypotheses.mechanism: Iteration 18 result (Cs+/XPhos/valeronitrile = 72.89% vs 89.17% for DMF) demonstrates a 16% absolute yield penalty for nitrile solvents even with the optimal cation-ligand pair. This suggests DMF's amide oxygen provides unique coordination to Pd(II) intermediates that nitrile nitrogen cannot replicate. The pattern extends to butyl acetate (iteration 5: 5.47% with K+). DMF's strong Lewis basicity (DN=26.6) and dipolar aprotic character appear essential for stabilizing the polar transition state of the DAR reaction. Solvent substitution is not a viable optimization strategy.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Any non-DMF solvent with any cation-ligand combination will yield <80%, with nitriles achieving 70-78% and esters achieving <20%. Future iterations should fix solvent=DMF and explore only cation-ligand-concentration-temperature space.

hypotheses.text: The 0.057M/105°C conditions are robustly optimal across all viable cation-ligand combinations (Cs+ with XPhos/RuPhos); ligand-specific condition optimization is unnecessary
hypotheses.mechanism: Despite testing diverse ligands (XPhos, BrettPhos, tBu3P, PCy3, PPh3) and cations (Cs+, K+), the 0.057M/105°C conditions have consistently performed well when the cation-ligand pair is viable. The iteration 16 BrettPhos/Cs+ disaster (42%) was due to cation-ligand incompatibility, not suboptimal conditions. Temperature robustness is confirmed across 90-120°C [RULE:R5]. Concentration effects are secondary to cation-ligand matching. Attempting ligand-specific condition optimization (e.g., 0.1M/90°C for BrettPhos) is unlikely to rescue incompatible pairs and wastes iterations.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos/Cs+/DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve within 3% of its maximum possible yield; testing alternative conditions (0.1M/110°C or 0.05M/100°C) will show <2% variation. This validates fixing conditions at 0.057M/105°C for all remaining iterations, focusing exploration exclusively on cation-ligand combinations.

hypotheses.text: SMKBO with Tanimoto kernels for ligand/base/solvent SMILES and separate handling of continuous variables will resolve the 7-iteration GP failure and enable reliable prediction of cation-ligand interaction effects
hypotheses.mechanism: The current one-hot encoding (26D) with Matern-5/2 kernel assumes smoothness in a space where chemical similarity is destroyed. XPhos and BrettPhos are 'distant' in one-hot space despite sharing biaryl phosphine architecture. The kernel matrix becomes ill-conditioned with limited data (n=20), causing ModelFittingError. SMKBO uses Tanimoto similarity on molecular fingerprints, capturing that ligands with similar scaffolds have correlated performance. Separate kernels for cation, ligand, solvent, and continuous variables allow explicit modeling of interactions—critical since Cs+/BrettPhos (42%) and K+/BrettPhos (79%) show opposite trends to the general Cs+>K+ rule. The kernel structure encodes domain knowledge: cation-ligand compatibility is a learnable interaction term, not additive.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguration to SMKBO, the model will (a) successfully fit without ModelFittingError for 3+ consecutive iterations, (b) predict Cs+/BrettPhos/DMF yield <50% (capturing the incompatibility), (c) predict RuPhos/Cs+/DMF yield 85-92% (exploiting biaryl scaffold similarity to XPhos), (d) uncertainty estimates will be well-calibrated (predicted ±5% vs observed error).

hypotheses.text: RuPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) with Cs+ cation will achieve 90-95% yield in DMF at 0.057M/105°C, breaking the 89.17% barrier that has persisted for 12 iterations
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines the proven biaryl phosphine scaffold (shared with XPhos, BrettPhos) with 2,6-diisopropoxy substitution—electron-rich but sterically and electronically distinct from XPhos's isopropyl groups and BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups. The isopropoxy groups are less basic than dimethylamino, avoiding the Cs+ incompatibility that caused BrettPhos/Cs+ to crash to 42%. The alkoxy oxygen may provide hemilabile coordination stabilizing the Pd(II) intermediate during reductive elimination. The dicyclohexylphosphino group provides optimal steric bulk (cone angle ~170°) for the Diels-Alder transition state. Cs+ stabilizes the acetate base and Pd-enolate intermediate without the deleterious ligand-cation interactions observed with BrettPhos.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos/Cs+/acetate/DMF/0.057M/105°C will achieve 90-95% yield, exceeding the current 89.17% maximum. If yield is 85-89%, RuPhos matches but does not exceed XPhos. If <80%, the biaryl phosphine scaffold has reached its limit regardless of substitution pattern.

hypotheses.text: BrettPhos with K+ cation at non-optimal conditions (0.1M, 90°C) will achieve 85-90% yield, validating that BrettPhos/K+ has narrow condition sensitivity and the 79% result was suboptimal, not a fundamental limit
hypotheses.mechanism: The iteration 15 result (K+/BrettPhos/DMF/0.057M/105°C = 78.95%) used the same conditions as the Cs+/XPhos optimum, but BrettPhos/K+ may have different condition sensitivity. The dimethylamino groups on BrettPhos create a more electron-rich, sterically demanding environment that may require higher concentration (0.1M) for productive catalyst-substrate encounters or lower temperature (90°C) to prevent catalyst decomposition. K+ coordination to BrettPhos's amino groups may alter the optimal Pd oxidation state distribution, requiring condition tuning. This hypothesis tests whether BrettPhos/K+ can be rescued to competitive yields (85%+) or is fundamentally limited to <80%.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BrettPhos/K+/acetate/DMF/0.1M/90°C will achieve 85-90% yield, exceeding the 78.95% at standard conditions. If 75-85%, BrettPhos/K+ has modest condition sensitivity but cannot match Cs+ systems. If <75%, BrettPhos is strictly incompatible with K+ regardless of conditions.

hypotheses.text: N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) or dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as DMF alternatives will maintain >85% yield with Cs+/XPhos, providing process flexibility without yield penalty
hypotheses.mechanism: DMF's essentiality [RULE:R3] is driven by its polar aprotic character and Lewis basicity (DN=26.6), not specific amide functionality. NMP (DN=27.3, similar polarity) and DMSO (DN=29.8, stronger donor) share these properties and are common DMF substitutes in Pd-catalyzed reactions. The iteration 18 valeronitrile test (72.89% vs 89.17% DMF) showed nitriles are inferior, but amide-type solvents (NMP) or sulfoxides (DMSO) may match DMF. NMP's cyclic structure provides similar coordination geometry to DMF; DMSO's stronger donor ability may stabilize Pd(II) intermediates differently. Both solvents are compatible with Cs+ acetate base systems.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/XPhos/acetate/NMP/0.057M/105°C or Cs+/XPhos/acetate/DMSO/0.057M/105°C will achieve 85-90% yield, within 5% of the DMF optimum. If 75-85%, solvent class has moderate effect; if <75%, DMF's specific amide structure is essential.

hypotheses.text: The optimal concentration-temperature combination is ligand-dependent: XPhos prefers 0.057M/105°C, but BrettPhos and RuPhos have different optima that must be discovered independently
hypotheses.mechanism: The current 'universal' optimum (0.057M/105°C) was established with XPhos and may not transfer to other ligands. Different biaryl phosphines have distinct steric profiles (cone angles: XPhos ~175°, BrettPhos ~190°, RuPhos ~170°) and electronic properties (Hammett σp: XPhos ~-0.15, BrettPhos ~-0.35, RuPhos ~-0.10), affecting the rate-limiting step and thus condition sensitivity. BrettPhos's larger cone angle may require higher concentration to achieve productive collision frequency; RuPhos's smaller angle may tolerate lower concentration. Temperature affects catalyst lifetime differently for each ligand's Pd complex stability. Testing each ligand at 0.057M/105°C, 0.1M/105°C, 0.057M/90°C, and 0.1M/90°C will map condition-ligand coupling.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos/Cs+/DMF/0.1M/90°C will achieve higher yield than at 0.057M/105°C (current XPhos optimum), demonstrating ligand-specific condition optima. If all conditions yield similar results (<5% spread), conditions are truly transferable [RULE:R4,R5].

hypotheses.text: Switching from Matern-5/2 to RBF kernel will resolve the 7-iteration ModelFittingError by providing stronger smoothness regularization that stabilizes the ill-conditioned covariance matrix in the 26-dimensional one-hot space
hypotheses.mechanism: The Matern-5/2 kernel assumes twice-differentiable functions with moderate smoothness, which is inappropriate for the sparse, high-dimensional one-hot encoded space where chemical similarity is destroyed. RBF (squared exponential) kernel imposes infinite differentiability and stronger regularization, effectively smoothing over the discontinuities between categorical levels. This aggressive regularization may enable successful Cholesky decomposition of the kernel matrix despite limited data (n=21) and high dimensionality (d=26). The trade-off is potential underfitting of sharp interaction effects, but given complete model failure, any functional model is preferable.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguration to RBF kernel, the GP will successfully fit for 3+ consecutive iterations without ModelFittingError. Prediction MAE on the next 5 iterations will be <25% (vs >45% current). Specifically, the model will predict: (a) Cs+/XPhos/DMF ~85-90% (reasonable), (b) K+/any ligand <70% (capturing cation effect), (c) non-DMF solvents <75% (capturing solvent effect).

hypotheses.text: RuPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) with Cs+ cation in DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve 90-95% yield, breaking the 89.17% barrier established at iteration 8
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines the proven biaryl phosphine scaffold with 2,6-diisopropoxy substitution—electron-rich but lacking BrettPhos's deleterious dimethylamino groups that caused Cs+ incompatibility (iteration 16: 42%). The isopropoxy groups provide hemilabile oxygen coordination that may stabilize Pd(II) intermediates during reductive elimination. Cs+ stabilizes the acetate base without the ligand-cation antagonism observed with BrettPhos. DMF is mandatory per [RULE:R3]; 0.057M/105°C is validated optimum per [RULE:R4,R5]. This is the final unexplored biaryl phosphine with plausible path to >90%.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos/Cs+/acetate/DMF/0.057M/105°C will achieve 90-95% yield. If 85-89%, RuPhos matches XPhos but no breakthrough. If <80%, the biaryl phosphine scaffold has reached its limit at ~89% regardless of substitution, forcing pivot to substrate modification or campaign termination.

hypotheses.text: If RBF kernel fails to resolve ModelFittingError within 3 iterations, SMKBO with Tanimoto kernels for SMILES and structured cation-ligand interaction terms is mandatory for campaign continuation
hypotheses.mechanism: One-hot encoding fundamentally destroys chemical structure information—XPhos and BrettPhos are equidistant from PCy3 in 26D space despite sharing biaryl architecture. The kernel matrix becomes rank-deficient with n<d (21 observations, 26 dimensions). SMKBO uses molecular fingerprints with Tanimoto similarity, placing XPhos and BrettPhos in the same chemical neighborhood. Explicit interaction kernels between cation and ligand subspaces can learn that BrettPhos-Cs+ is antagonistic while XPhos-Cs+ is synergistic. This is not merely a kernel choice but an architectural change encoding domain knowledge.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: If RBF fails (ModelFittingError persists iterations 22-24), SMKBO reconfiguration will: (a) enable successful fitting within 2 iterations, (b) reduce prediction error to <15% MAE, (c) correctly rank-order unexplored ligands by predicted yield. If SMKBO also fails, the campaign must abandon BO for pure chemical reasoning.

hypotheses.text: N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) or dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) will maintain >85% yield with Cs+/XPhos, providing viable DMF alternatives for process flexibility
hypotheses.mechanism: DMF's essentiality [RULE:R3] derives from its polar aprotic character (ε=36.7) and Lewis basicity (DN=26.6), not specific amide functionality. NMP (ε=32.0, DN=27.3) and DMSO (ε=46.7, DN=29.8) share these properties. Iteration 18 showed valeronitrile (nitrile solvent) is inferior (72.89% vs 89.17%), but amide-type (NMP) and sulfoxide (DMSO) solvents may match DMF. NMP's cyclic structure mimics DMF's coordination geometry; DMSO's stronger donor ability may alter Pd(II) stabilization. Both are compatible with Cs+ acetate systems.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/XPhos/acetate/NMP/0.057M/105°C or Cs+/XPhos/acetate/DMSO/0.057M/105°C will achieve 85-90% yield. If 75-85%, solvent class has moderate effect; if <75%, DMF's specific amide structure is essential and cannot be substituted.

hypotheses.text: If RuPhos fails to achieve >85% and RBF kernel does not restore model functionality, the campaign should terminate at iteration 25 with 89.17% as the practical maximum, avoiding wasted budget on non-functional optimization
hypotheses.mechanism: The campaign has consumed 52.5% of budget (21/40 iterations) with 13 iterations of stagnation. The 89.17% optimum was established at iteration 8 and has not been improved despite extensive exploration. All high-potential ligands (XPhos optimized, BrettPhos failed, monodentates eliminated) have been tested except RuPhos. If RuPhos fails and the model remains non-functional, remaining iterations will be random exploration with <10% probability of improvement. Chemical reasoning has exhausted the searchable space; further iterations waste resources without information gain.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: If iterations 22-25 show: (a) RuPhos yield <80%, (b) continued ModelFittingError with RBF kernel, (c) no candidates >85% predicted yield, then termination at iteration 25 with final report is optimal. Expected value of continuing: <2% probability of >90% yield; expected value of termination: conserved resources, clear conclusion.

hypotheses.text: Switching from Matern-5/2 to RBF kernel will resolve the 7-iteration ModelFittingError by providing stronger smoothness regularization that stabilizes the ill-conditioned covariance matrix in the 26-dimensional one-hot space
hypotheses.mechanism: The Matern-5/2 kernel assumes twice-differentiable functions with moderate smoothness, which is inappropriate for the sparse, high-dimensional one-hot encoded space where chemical similarity is destroyed. RBF (squared exponential) kernel imposes infinite differentiability and stronger regularization, effectively smoothing over the discontinuities between categorical levels. This aggressive regularization may enable successful Cholesky decomposition of the kernel matrix despite limited data (n=21) and high dimensionality (d=26). The trade-off is potential underfitting of sharp interaction effects, but given complete model failure, any functional model is preferable.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguration to RBF kernel, the GP will successfully fit for 3+ consecutive iterations without ModelFittingError, and prediction errors will decrease from 60+ points (iteration 21: predicted 65.7% vs actual 4.89%) to <30 points within 5 iterations.

hypotheses.text: RuPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) with Cs+ cation in DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve 90-95% yield, breaking the 89.17% XPhos barrier by avoiding BrettPhos's amino-group-Cs+ incompatibility
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines the proven biaryl phosphine scaffold with 2,6-diisopropoxy substitution instead of BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups. The alkoxy groups provide electron-rich character for catalyst stabilization without the Lewis basicity that disrupts Cs+ coordination in BrettPhos. This preserves the biaryl framework's steric and electronic advantages while eliminating the specific incompatibility that caused iteration 16's 42.43% disaster. The hemilabile alkoxy coordination may provide additional catalyst stability during the catalytic cycle.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/RuPhos/DMF/0.057M/105°C will achieve >90% yield, exceeding the current 89.17% optimum. If RuPhos is unavailable in the dataset, SPhos (dimethoxy analogue) will achieve 85-90% as a fallback.

hypotheses.text: Cation-ligand compatibility is determined by ligand topology: Cs+ is compatible with biaryl phosphines bearing alkyl/alkoxy ortho-substituents (XPhos, RuPhos) but incompatible with amino-substituted biaryls (BrettPhos); K+ is incompatible with all biaryl phosphines regardless of substitution
hypotheses.mechanism: The Cs+ cation requires specific coordination geometry at the Pd center that is disrupted by the Lewis basic dimethylamino groups in BrettPhos but tolerated by alkyl/alkoxy groups in XPhos and RuPhos. The amino groups may compete with substrate/cation for Pd coordination or alter the electronic environment of the catalytic center unfavorably. K+ is fundamentally incompatible with biaryl phosphine catalysts due to ionic radius and coordination preferences, explaining the consistent K+ underperformance (max 78.95% vs 89.17% for Cs+/XPhos).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos/Cs+ will achieve >85% yield; BrettPhos/Cs+ will remain <50% even with optimized conditions; all K+/biaryl combinations will remain <80%. This pattern will hold across solvent and concentration variations.

hypotheses.text: When the GP model generates candidates violating >2 established chemical rules with >90% confidence each, automatic iteration halt and manual reconfiguration is superior to proceeding with the flawed candidate or falling back to random selection
hypotheses.mechanism: Iteration 21's disaster (4.89% yield, predicted 65.7%) occurred because the model-generated shortlist violated R3 (DMF essential), R6 (Cs+ preferred), and H3 (BrettPhos/Cs+ incompatible) simultaneously. The fallback to shortlist index 0 when the override candidate was unavailable wasted budget on a known-bad experiment. A hard stop with reconfiguration demand would have preserved the iteration for RuPhos testing (H2). This hypothesis addresses the agent's decision-making failure, not chemical mechanism.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Implementation of a rule-violation counter with threshold=2 and automatic halt will prevent future iteration 21-type disasters. The cost of delayed iteration (0) is less than cost of bad experiment (negative information, wasted budget).

hypotheses.text: If RBF kernel fails to resolve ModelFittingError within 3 iterations, switching from one-hot to SMKBO (structured molecular kernel Bayesian optimization) with Tanimoto fingerprint kernels is required for campaign continuation
hypotheses.mechanism: One-hot encoding destroys chemical structure information, creating a 26-dimensional space where similar molecules (e.g., XPhos and RuPhos) are equidistant from dissimilar molecules (e.g., XPhos and PCy3). This structural blindness causes kernel matrix ill-conditioning because the GP cannot exploit chemical similarity priors. SMKBO with Tanimoto kernels on SMILES fingerprints encodes structural similarity explicitly, reducing effective dimensionality and enabling meaningful covariance structure even with limited data. The molecular kernel captures scaffold similarities (biaryl phosphines cluster together) and substitution patterns.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: If RBF kernel fails (ModelFittingError persists for iterations 22-24), SMKBO reconfiguration will enable successful model fitting by iteration 25. Prediction accuracy will improve because structurally similar ligands will have correlated predictions.

hypotheses.text: Reconfiguring from one-hot encoding to SMKBO with Tanimoto kernels on Morgan fingerprints will resolve the 9-iteration ModelFittingError and enable reliable prediction of cation-ligand interaction effects
hypotheses.mechanism: One-hot encoding creates a 26-dimensional space where chemical similarity is destroyed—XPhos and RuPhos (both biaryl phosphines) are as 'distant' as XPhos and PCy3 (completely different scaffold). This causes kernel matrix ill-conditioning with limited data (n=22), triggering persistent Cholesky decomposition failures. SMKBO with Tanimoto similarity on molecular fingerprints captures structural relationships: biaryl phosphines cluster together, enabling transfer learning from XPhos to RuPhos. Separate treatment of cation, ligand, solvent, and continuous variables allows explicit modeling of the Cs+-BrettPhos incompatibility that one-hot encoding cannot learn. The Tanimoto kernel's bounded similarity [0,1] provides better numerical stability than unbounded distance metrics.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After SMKBO reconfiguration: (a) Model will fit successfully for 5+ consecutive iterations without ModelFittingError; (b) Prediction MAE on held-out combinations will improve from >40% (current) to <20%; (c) Model will correctly predict K+ combinations <75%, BrettPhos/Cs+ <50%, RuPhos/Cs+ 85-92%; (d) Uncertainty estimates will be well-calibrated (coverage 80-90%).

hypotheses.text: RuPhos (2-dicyclohexylphosphino-2',6'-diisopropoxybiphenyl) with Cs+ cation in DMF at 0.057M/105°C will achieve 88-94% yield, matching or exceeding the current 89.17% XPhos optimum
hypotheses.mechanism: RuPhos combines the proven biaryl phosphine scaffold with 2,6-diisopropoxy substitution, avoiding BrettPhos's dimethylamino groups that caused Cs+ incompatibility (iteration 16: 42.43%). The alkoxy groups provide electron-rich character for catalyst stabilization without Lewis basicity that disrupts Pd coordination geometry. Tanimoto similarity between XPhos and RuPhos (both biaryl with ortho-substitution) predicts similar performance; SMKBO will exploit this structural relationship to guide selection. Hemilabile alkoxy coordination may provide additional catalyst stability. This is the highest-priority chemical hypothesis with untested >90% potential.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/RuPhos/DMF/0.057M/105°C will yield 88-94%. If RuPhos unavailable, SPhos (dimethoxy analogue, Tanimoto similarity ~0.85 to RuPhos) will achieve 85-90%. Success validates alkoxy-biaryl-Cs+ compatibility; failure (<80%) indicates intrinsic biaryl phosphine limitations with Cs+.

hypotheses.text: Cation-ligand compatibility follows a strict matching rule: Cs+ requires alkyl/alkoxy-substituted biaryl phosphines (XPhos, RuPhos, SPhos); K+ is capped at ~80% regardless of ligand; amino-substituted biaryls (BrettPhos) are incompatible with both cations for high yields
hypotheses.mechanism: The iteration 16-22 disasters establish that cation-ligand pairing is discrete, not additive. BrettPhos/Cs+ (42.43%) << BrettPhos/K+ (78.95%) despite general Cs+>K+ trend, indicating amino-group-Cs+ incompatibility. XPhos/Cs+ (89.17%) >> XPhos/K+ (65.37%), confirming alkyl-substitution-Cs+ synergy. K+ consistently underperforms (max 78.95% vs 89.17% Cs+) due to ionic radius and coordination preferences incompatible with biaryl phosphine geometry. Monodentate phosphines fail with both cations (tBu3P/K+=0%, PCy3/K+=7.26%). This matching rule is structural: biaryl scaffold + alkyl/alkoxy ortho-substituents + Cs+ = optimal; any deviation reduces yield.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing RuPhos/Cs+ (predicted 88-94%), SPhos/Cs+ (predicted 85-90%), and reconfirming BrettPhos/K+ (predicted 75-80%) will validate the rule. If SPhos/Cs+ <75%, rule is specific to isopropyl/alkoxy substitution; if all alkyl/alkoxy biaryls succeed, rule generalizes to scaffold-electronics matching.

hypotheses.text: Concentration 0.057M and DMF solvent are near-optimal across all compatible cation-ligand combinations, with <5% yield variation for 0.05-0.1M and polar aprotic solvents (DMF, NMP, DMSO)
hypotheses.mechanism: Rules R3 (DMF essential, confidence 0.92) and R4 (concentration=0.057 positive) were established with Cs+/XPhos. The iteration 18 valeronitrile test (72.89% vs 89.17% DMF) showed nitriles are inferior, but amide-type solvents (NMP) or sulfoxides (DMSO) may match DMF. The 0.057M optimum likely transfers to RuPhos/Cs+ due to similar biaryl scaffold and reaction mechanism. Testing NMP or DMSO with proven Cs+/XPhos can validate solvent class flexibility without risking ligand optimization. Concentration robustness (0.05-0.1M) enables process flexibility for scale-up.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cs+/XPhos/NMP/0.057M/105°C or Cs+/XPhos/DMSO/0.057M/105°C will achieve 85-90% yield (within 5% of DMF). Cs+/RuPhos/DMF/0.1M/105°C will achieve 85-90% (concentration tolerance). If <80%, conditions are ligand-specific and require re-optimization per ligand.

hypotheses.mechanism: The campaign has wasted 20% of budget (iterations 15-22) on model-failure-driven random exploration. Current best (89.17%) has held for 14 iterations. SMKBO reconfiguration is mandatory for any model-guided progress. RuPhos is the only unexplored ligand with >90% potential. If RuPhos fails, no other ligand class offers breakthrough potential—SPhos is similar to XPhos, BrettPhos is eliminated, monodentates are eliminated. After SMKBO reconfiguration + RuPhos test + 3 confirmation iterations (total 5 iterations), if no improvement beyond 89.17%, the campaign should accept current optimum and terminate to preserve remaining budget for other substrates or conditions.

This causes kernel matrix ill-conditioning with limited data (n=22), triggering persistent Cholesky decomposition failures. SMKBO with Tanimoto similarity on molecular fingerprints captures structural relationships: biaryl phosphines cluster together, enabling transfer learning from XPhos to RuPhos. Separate treatment of cation, ligand, solvent, and continuous variables allows explicit modeling of the Cs+-BrettPhos incompatibility that one-hot encoding cannot learn.
